bins.name: Standardize Study Process Nationally
bins.desc: iMasons argues that the current fragmented, state-by-utility process produces opaque rules, inaccurate load forecasts, and inefficient infrastructure investment. The final rule should set minimum national standards with consistent technical requirements developed in coordination with NERC.
quotes: The United States' current large load interconnection landscape consists of a disjointed, state-by-state, and in some instances, utility-by-utility, set of rules and processes that are often opaque and routinely in flux.

maintaining the status quo with respect to large load interconnections will prevent the United States from building out data centers at the pace and scale needed to win the AI race and stay competitive globally, which will undermine the country's short and long-term economic, national security and scientific interests.

the lack of coordination with regard to large load interconnection processes leads to inaccurate load forecasts, resulting in, among other things, the inefficient buildout of generation and transmission to serve load.

interconnecting utilities should be required to provide large load developers with: 1) sufficient information regarding anticipated state-jurisdictional and FERC-jurisdictional costs that they will be expected to bear, including a clear understanding of how FERC-jurisdictional costs are expected to be reflected in retail rates; and 2) drafts of all relevant agreements that they will need to execute to interconnect their projects.

iMasons urges the Commission to work with NERC and transmission providers to expedite the development of clear and consistent technical standards that will govern the interconnection of large loads nationally.

bins.name: Cost Causation Must Govern Allocation
bins.desc: iMasons consistently invokes the Commission's cost causation principle to argue that deposits, penalties, upgrade cost assignments, and revenue guarantees must all be tied to costs that large load customers actually cause. Revenue guarantee constructs like Exelon's TSA framework are singled out as violations of this principle.
quotes: the lack of coordination with regard to large load interconnection processes leads to inaccurate load forecasts, resulting in, among other things, the inefficient buildout of generation and transmission to serve load.
Deposits and penalties should be fashioned in a manner that mitigates risks and costs that large loads actually pose to transmission owners and other customers.

Requirements that are disproportionate to the risk and costs that large loads actually pose to transmission owners and other customers do not help protect consumers, and instead, overcompensate utilities and risk stifling the market for data centers in the United States.

the TSA construct, and any similar 'revenue guarantee' proposals that may be proposed by other transmission owners, would clearly run afoul of the Commission's cost causation principal, which requires 'that all approved rates reflect to some degree the costs actually caused by the customer [that] must pay them.'
the Commission should not permit utilities to 'claw back' capacity that has already been secured by a large load developer or hyperscale end-user.

Encouraging hybrid facilities will help solve 'speed to power' issues for data centers, enhance reliability by encouraging data centers to bring their own generation when they interconnect and can alleviate the need for building excess network upgrades, which will benefit consumers.

iMasons encourages the Commission to promulgate a final rule that would require large load customers to be allocated only costs that they cause, consistent with the Commission's longstanding cost causation principles.

bins.name: Deposits Tied to Actual Risk
bins.desc: iMasons supports standardized deposits and withdrawal penalties only when they are proportionate to actual risks posed to transmission owners. It opposes revenue guarantee frameworks that require large financial commitments regardless of actual costs incurred, and opposes utility capacity clawbacks.
quotes: Deposits and penalties should be fashioned in a manner that mitigates risks and costs that large loads actually pose to transmission owners and other customers.

Requirements that are disproportionate to the risk and costs that large loads actually pose to transmission owners and other customers do not help protect consumers, and instead, overcompensate utilities and risk stifling the market for data centers in the United States.

the TSA construct, and any similar 'revenue guarantee' proposals that may be proposed by other transmission owners, would clearly run afoul of the Commission's cost causation principal, which requires 'that all approved rates reflect to some degree the costs actually caused by the customer [that] must pay them.'
the Commission should not permit utilities to 'claw back' capacity that has already been secured by a large load developer or hyperscale end-user.

bins.name: Support Joint Study of Hybrid Facilities
bins.desc: iMasons supports studying hybrid load-and-generation facilities together, based on their actual injection and withdrawal rights. This approach can reduce network upgrade costs and address the speed-to-power problem for data centers.
quotes: iMasons supports studying hybrid facilities together with generating facilities to the greatest extent practical, and studying hybrid facilities based on their requested amount of injection and/or withdrawal rights.

Encouraging hybrid facilities will help solve 'speed to power' issues for data centers, enhance reliability by encouraging data centers to bring their own generation when they interconnect and can alleviate the need for building excess network upgrades, which will benefit consumers.

bins.name: Hybrid Frameworks Benefit Consumers
bins.desc: iMasons endorses bring-your-own-generation frameworks on a voluntary basis, noting that co-located generation enhances reliability and reduces upgrade needs. However, these frameworks should not disrupt existing generator interconnection queues.
quotes: iMasons supports studying hybrid facilities together with generating facilities to the greatest extent practical, and studying hybrid facilities based on their requested amount of injection and/or withdrawal rights.

Encouraging hybrid facilities will help solve 'speed to power' issues for data centers, enhance reliability by encouraging data centers to bring their own generation when they interconnect and can alleviate the need for building excess network upgrades, which will benefit consumers.

bins.name: Accelerate Flexible Load Interconnection
bins.desc: iMasons supports faster interconnection for loads that voluntarily accept curtailment or dispatch, while protecting parties already in existing queues from disruption.
quotes: iMasons supports a final rule that accelerates the interconnection of large loads that voluntarily agree to be curtailable and dispatchable (i.e., flexible), provided that existing interconnection queues are not unduly and unjustly disrupted.

bins.name: Expedited Process for Flexible Loads
bins.desc: iMasons backs expedited interconnection treatment for large flexible loads as a way to address speed-to-power concerns for data centers without burdening the broader interconnection queue.
quotes: iMasons supports a final rule that accelerates the interconnection of large loads that voluntarily agree to be curtailable and dispatchable (i.e., flexible), provided that existing interconnection queues are not unduly and unjustly disrupted.

bins.name: Assign Only Costs Customers Cause
bins.desc: iMasons takes no position on whether network upgrade costs should be directly assigned or rolled into rate structures, but insists that whatever method is used must ensure large load customers pay only their fair share of caused costs.
quotes: iMasons encourages the Commission to promulgate a final rule that would require large load customers to be allocated only costs that they cause, consistent with the Commission's longstanding cost causation principles.

bins.name: NERC Coordination on Technical Standards
bins.desc: iMasons urges FERC to work with NERC and transmission providers to establish clear, consistent national technical standards for large load interconnection to reduce project delays and reliability risks.
quotes: iMasons urges the Commission to work with NERC and transmission providers to expedite the development of clear and consistent technical standards that will govern the interconnection of large loads nationally.

bins.name: Raise Threshold to 75 MW
bins.desc: iMasons argues that the ANOPR's proposed 20 MW threshold is too low given that modern data centers often exceed hundreds of MWs or even 1 GW, and recommends a minimum of 75 MW or higher.
quotes: iMasons believes that a minimum threshold of 75 MW (or potentially higher) is appropriate for the Commission to consider.

bins.name: Fragmented Rules Hinder Development
bins.desc: iMasons characterizes the current landscape as a labyrinth of opaque, inconsistent state and utility rules that slow project development and create uncertainty for developers.
quotes: The United States' current large load interconnection landscape consists of a disjointed, state-by-state, and in some instances, utility-by-utility, set of rules and processes that are often opaque and routinely in flux.

bins.name: AI Race Requires Faster Buildout
bins.desc: iMasons frames the urgency of interconnection reform in terms of national competitiveness, arguing that slow interconnection processes risk driving data center investment abroad and undermining U.S. economic and security interests.
quotes: maintaining the status quo with respect to large load interconnections will prevent the United States from building out data centers at the pace and scale needed to win the AI race and stay competitive globally, which will undermine the country's short and long-term economic, national security and scientific interests.

bins.name: Upfront Cost and Agreement Disclosure
bins.desc: iMasons asks that utilities be required to give developers early access to anticipated costs (state and FERC jurisdictional) and draft agreements, so developers can make informed decisions and speculative projects exit queues sooner.
quotes: interconnecting utilities should be required to provide large load developers with: 1) sufficient information regarding anticipated state-jurisdictional and FERC-jurisdictional costs that they will be expected to bear, including a clear understanding of how FERC-jurisdictional costs are expected to be reflected in retail rates; and 2) drafts of all relevant agreements that they will need to execute to interconnect their projects.

bins.name: Revenue Guarantee Frameworks Oppose
bins.desc: iMasons singles out the Exelon TSA revenue guarantee construct as going far beyond protecting utilities, requiring large credit support unrelated to actual abandonment costs, and likely chilling investment by independent data center developers.
quotes: the TSA construct, and any similar 'revenue guarantee' proposals that may be proposed by other transmission owners, would clearly run afoul of the Commission's cost causation principal, which requires 'that all approved rates reflect to some degree the costs actually caused by the customer [that] must pay them.'

bins.name: Prospective Rules with Grandfathering
bins.desc: iMasons urges a clear transition mechanism so any new rules apply prospectively and projects with substantial sunk costs or binding equipment commitments can be grandfathered under existing processes.
quotes: iMasons supports a final rule that establishes a clear transition mechanism, and is effective on a prospective basis only, so that any existing large load projects already in development are not disrupted.
